story_clusters.headline: Assembly elections: Results in four States, U.T. triumph of democracy and Constitution, says Modi
story_clusters.summary: The Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) has secured victories in multiple state elections, including West Bengal and Tamil Nadu, while retaining Assam. In West Bengal, the BJP's success is attributed to strong support from the Matua community, and in Tamil Nadu, actor Vijay’s Tamilaga Vettri Kazhagam (TVK) was involved in the political shift. The BJP-led NDA has also formed governments in all Ganga-bank states.

The country has also unveiled a new map asserting control over the Strait of Hormuz under its armed forces, while warning the U.S. Navy to avoid the area following reports of U.S. plans to assist stranded ships. Additionally, Iran has threatened a "kinetic response" to the U.S.'s "Project Freedom" naval mission in the region.

story_clusters.summary: SEBI has proposed amendments to its securitisation directions to align with RBI’s securitisation framework amid conflicts between existing rules. Additionally, SEBI’s chair noted that RBI and IRDAI are not inclined to permit banks and insurers to participate in commodity derivatives.

articles.title: PF/ESI Addition Quashed: CPC Cannot Decide Debatable Issues Under Section 143(1)
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) quashed additions made by the Centralized Processing Centre (CPC) regarding employee contributions (PF/ESI) during return processing. The tribunal ruled that CPC cannot decide debatable issues under Section 143(1) and directed the deletion of such additions. This ruling impacts tax compliance and employee benefit deductions.
articles.raw_summary: The case involved disallowance of employee contributions during return processing. The Tribunal held such adjustments invalid on debatable issues and directed deletion of additions. articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled on April 6, 2026, that criminal proceedings can be quashed if reliable evidence disproves allegations, undermining the complaint's factual foundation. The case, SAJAL BOSE VERSUS THE STATE OF WEST BENGAL AND ORS., involved a bench of Justices Vikram Nath and Sandeep Mehta. This decision strengthens judicial oversight in criminal cases, ensuring fair proceedings under the BNSS. The ruling has implications for due process and the rights of accused individuals.

articles.title: GST Law Requires Year-Wise Notices, Bombay HC Strikes Down Multi-Year SCN
articles.summary: The Bombay High Court ruled that the Goods and Services Tax (GST) law does not permit combining multiple financial years into a single show cause notice (SCN). The court held that each financial year must be treated as a separate tax period, striking down the practice of multi-year SCNs. This decision impacts how tax authorities issue notices for GST compliance. The ruling emphasizes strict adherence to year-wise notices under GST law.
articles.raw_summary: The case addressed whether multiple financial years can be combined in a single show cause notice under GST law. The Court held such consolidation impermissible, emphasizing that each financial year is a separate tax period. articles.title: SC allowed Pre-Deposit GST Refund Due to Inapplicability of Section 54 Limitation
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that the two-year limitation period under Section 54 of the GST law is directory (not mandatory) and cannot block legitimate refund claims. The dispute involved whether the time limit should strictly apply, and the Court's decision allows flexibility in deserving cases. This ruling impacts how tax refunds are processed under GST, ensuring fairness for taxpayers.
articles.raw_summary: The dispute concerned whether the two-year limit under Section 54 is mandatory. The Court ruled it is directory and cannot defeat legitimate refund claims. articles.title: Share Premium Tax Above FMV: End of Section 56(2)(viib), Section 92(2)(m) Continues, Tax Gap Emerges
articles.summary: The Indian government has removed Section 56(2)(viib) of the Income Tax Act, ending the tax on share premiums exceeding fair market value. This change affects companies and investors, as they will no longer face taxation on such share premiums. While Section 92(2)(m) of the Income Tax Act continues, a tax gap has emerged due to this policy shift. The move is expected to impact fundraising and investment strategies in India.
articles.raw_summary: The removal of the provision means companies and investors are no longer taxed on share premiums exceeding fair market value, creating a major policy shift. The post Share Premium Tax Above FMV: End of Section 56(2)(viib), Section 92(2)(m) Continues, Tax Gap Emerges appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Kerala Assembly Elections 2026: CPI suffers major setback, seat count halved
articles.summary: The CPI party in Kerala suffered a significant setback in the 2026 Assembly Elections, losing half of its seats compared to 2021 when it had won 17 seats. articles.title: Bridging Urban Data Gaps for Effective Policy Interventions
articles.summary: The Observer Research Foundation highlights the lack of comprehensive urban data in India, which hinders effective policy-making for cities. This data gap affects urban planning, infrastructure development, and service delivery, making it difficult to design targeted interventions. Addressing this issue could improve governance and resource allocation in India's rapidly growing urban areas.

articles.title: DEA eases FDI rules for firms with up to 10% Chinese shareholding under FEMA
articles.summary: The Directorate of Enforcement (DEA) has relaxed foreign direct investment (FDI) rules for companies with up to 10% Chinese shareholding under the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA). The revised policy focuses on beneficial ownership while maintaining restrictions on entities from land-border countries, signaling a calibrated easing of post-Covid FDI regulations.

articles.title: Article 226 Can't Be Invoked To Seek FIR Registration Without Availing Statutory Remedies : Supreme Court
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that Article 226 of the Indian Constitution cannot be used to seek the registration of an FIR without first exhausting statutory remedies. The Court emphasized that Article 226 is not a universal solution for all grievances. This ruling clarifies the limits of judicial intervention in law enforcement matters.

The decision underscores the importance of adhering to statutory procedures rather than seeking judicial intervention prematurely.

articles.title: Routine BPO Comparable Excluded for Functional Difference Reason; Different Year Comparable Considered on Quarterly Data Basis
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal ruled that a company providing high-end knowledge process outsourcing (KPO) and business solutions cannot be compared to a routine captive business process outsourcing (BPO) service provider due to functional differences. The tribunal also held that a comparable cannot be rejected solely because it follows a different financial year if reliable quarterly data is available. The decision impacts how companies are assessed for tax purposes in India.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal held that a company engaged in high-end KPO and business solutions could not be compared with a routine captive BPO service provider. It also ruled that a comparable cannot be rejected solely for following a different financial year if reliable quarterly data is available. articles.summary: The Telangana High Court allowed a manual revocation of GST registration for a taxpayer whose accountant failed to file returns for six months, despite the cancellation being initially time-barred. This ruling addresses procedural fairness in tax compliance and highlights judicial intervention in administrative delays.

articles.title: GST General Penalty Invalid Due to Prior Levy of Section 47 Late Fee: Madras HC
articles.summary: The Madras High Court ruled that imposing both a late fee under Section 47 and a general penalty for the same GST default is unjustified. The Court held that overlapping penalties are unsustainable and clarified that only one penal consequence can apply in such cases. This decision impacts tax compliance and enforcement under India's GST framework.
articles.raw_summary: The case examined the legality of imposing both late fee and general penalty for the same default. articles.title: U.S. and Iran truce tested over the Strait of Hormuz, threatening to reignite conflict
articles.summary: The fragile truce between the U.S. and Iran was challenged when American ships clearing a lane in the Strait of Hormuz were fired upon and sank Iranian boats. Iran also launched drones and missiles at a key oil terminal in the UAE, causing market jitters and raising fears of renewed conflict. The incident threatens global oil supplies and regional stability, with potential ripple effects on India's energy security and diplomatic stance.
articles.raw_summary: The tenuous truce between the U.S. and Iran was tested Monday, as American ships clearing a lane in the Strait of Hormuz came under fire and sank Iranian boats. Iran also fired drones and missiles at a key oil terminal in the United Arab Emirates, spooking markets and raising concerns that war could resume. Nick Schifrin reports.

articles.title: Iran war: UAE reports first Iranian missile and drone attacks in weeks
articles.summary: The United Arab Emirates reported missile and drone attacks from Iran, the first such incidents since April. The attacks coincide with U.S. efforts to secure shipping routes through the Strait of Hormuz, a critical oil transit chokepoint. The conflict raises concerns about regional stability and energy supply disruptions, which could impact India's oil imports and diplomatic engagements.
articles.raw_summary: The United Arab Emirates has reported missile and drone attacks from Iran, the first of their kind since the April ceasefire. This comes as the US seeks to help ships through the Strait of Hormuz.

articles.title: Why Russia's Northern Sea Route is a risk for global trade
articles.summary: Russia aims to develop the Northern Sea Route as a key global trade artery between Europe and Asia. However, the Arctic route faces significant political and environmental challenges. The development could disrupt existing trade patterns and energy supply chains, affecting countries like India that rely on maritime trade and energy imports.
articles.raw_summary: Russia wants its Northern Sea Route to become an important artery of global trade. However, the Arctic shortcut between Europe and Asia is fraught with both political and environmental hurdles.

The Left Democratic Front (LDF) is projected at 58–68 seats, while the National Democratic Alliance (NDA) is estimated to win 0–4 seats. The tight race indicates a possible shift in power dynamics that could impact future state policies.

articles.title: Supreme Court Clarifies: Section 480(3) BNSS Bail Condition Not Applicable To Offences Punishable Up To Seven Years
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled on 22 April 2026 that the mandatory bail conditions under Section 480(3) of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS), 2023, do not apply to offences punishable with up to seven years of imprisonment. The decision in Narayan v. State of Madhya Pradesh (SLP (Crl.) No. 7011 of 2026) clarifies a legal ambiguity that had been misapplied by trial courts since the BNSS came into force. This ruling impacts bail procedures nationwide and corrects a widespread judicial error.
articles.raw_summary: In a significant order passed on 22 nd April 2026, the Supreme Court settled an important question of bail jurisprudence that trial court across country had been getting wrong since Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (BNSS) came into force. In Narayan v. State of Madhya Pradesh, SLP (Crl.) No. 7011 of 2026 , a division bench comprising Justice J.K. Maheshwari and Atul S. Chandurkar held that the mandatory conditions prescribed under Section 480 (3) BNSS do not apply to non-bailable offence

articles.title: Kerala Assembly polls 2026: UDF win was made massive by anti-Left voting
articles.summary: The United Democratic Front (UDF) won the Kerala Assembly polls in 2026 decisively, with leaders attributing the victory to teamwork, election promises, and the Left Democratic Front (LDF) government's alleged misgovernance and nepotism. The LDF suffered heavy losses in traditional strongholds like Taliparamba, Payyannur, and Ambalapuzha, where UDF-backed CPI(M) rebels played a key role in the defeats.
articles.raw_summary: Senior leaders V.D. Satheesan, Ramesh Chennithala, and K.C. Venugopal attribute the massive win to cohesive teamwork within the alliance, the guarantees and assurances given to the people, methodical electioneering, and the ‘misgovernance and nepotism’ of the outgoing LDF government. The scale of the Left’s defeat has been amplified by the loss of face in some of its strongholds such as Taliparamba, Payyannur, and Ambalapuzha, where CPI(M) rebels backed by the UDF posted decisive victories

The case highlights the importance of accounting methods in tax assessments for businesses engaged in imports and exports.

Growth forecasts have been revised downward, while inflation expectations have risen due to higher energy costs. The situation highlights the vulnerability of energy-importing nations like India to global oil shocks.

Growth forecasts have been cut while inflation expectations have increased, reflecting higher energy prices.

articles.title: Registration Rejected for “Low Activity”? ITAT Sends It Back – Opportunity Must Precede Denial
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) set aside a registration rejection for 'low activity,' ruling that the tax authority failed to properly assess the genuineness of activities. The tribunal accepted the delay caused by the representative's illness as a valid reason and restored the matter for fresh adjudication. This decision highlights the need for authorities to provide opportunities before denying registrations. The case was remanded to the original authority for reconsideration.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal accepted the delay caused by representative’s illness as sufficient reason and admitted the appeal. articles.title: Factory Shift ≠ Capital Asset – ITAT Allows ₹1.13 Cr as Revenue, Calls Out “Enduring Benefit” Overreach
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) ruled that expenses incurred for shifting a factory do not qualify as capital assets, allowing a company to claim ₹1.13 crore as a revenue expense. The tribunal emphasized that the relocation did not enhance the business's structural assets, distinguishing it from capital expenditure. This decision clarifies tax treatment for operational expenses versus long-term investments, impacting corporate tax planning and compliance in India.
articles.raw_summary: The relocation did not lead to structural enhancement of business assets. The Tribunal ruled that such expenses remain in the revenue field. articles.title: Madras HC: Customs Broker Not to Be Penalised for Mis-Description While Filing Shipping Bills
articles.summary: The Madras High Court ruled that customs brokers cannot be penalized under Section 114AA of the Customs Act, 1962 for mis-description or wrong classification in shipping bills filed for MEIS scrips. articles.title: The Plant Fibre Sector Keeps Chasing Visibility When It Needs Markets
articles.summary: The plant fibre sector in India is gaining attention as industries seek bio-based materials with lower environmental impact. However, output remains low due to fragmented supply chains, weak processing capacity, and uncertain demand. A FIBRAL report suggests that diversification will require standards, investment, and market architecture, not just increased fibre production. The sector's struggles highlight potential areas for policy intervention to boost sustainable alternatives.
articles.raw_summary: As industries seek bio-based materials with lower environmental impact, alternative plant fibres are drawing new policy and commercial attention. Yet output remains marginal and forecast to contract, constrained by fragmented chains, weak processing capacity and uncertain offtake. A FIBRAL report argues diversification will depend on standards, investment and market architecture, not fibre discovery alone at scale.

articles.title: Direct tax mop-up misses FY26 target; Collections rise 5%

A clearer picture will emerge by June.

articles.title: Teen rape survivor’s choice vs unborn baby’s right to life—how SC’s new order deviates from precedent
articles.summary: The Supreme Court, led by Chief Justice of India Surya Kant, rejected AIIMS' plea to deny a 15-year-old rape survivor the right to terminate her 27-week pregnancy. Earlier court rulings often blocked terminations if the fetus was healthy, but this order deviates from precedent. The decision highlights the conflict between a survivor's choice and the unborn baby's right to life. The case underscores the need for clearer legal frameworks on abortion rights in India.
articles.raw_summary: SC bench led by CJI Surya Kant rejected AIIMS plea against allowing a 15-year-old girl to terminate her 27-week pregnancy. Earlier rulings often denied termination if foetus was in good health.

articles.title: Net direct tax collection up 5% in FY26, misses target
articles.summary: Government's net direct tax collections for FY26 reached over Rs 23.4 lakh crore, a 5.1% increase, though it fell short of the revised target. articles.title: Telangana govt to 'hand over' probe report on 'irregularities' in PPAs during BRS regime to CBI
articles.summary: The Telangana government will submit a report on alleged irregularities in Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) from the previous BRS regime to the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) for further investigation. articles.title: Section 54 Exemption not eligible on Sale of Open Land Without Building: ITAT Allahabad
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) in Allahabad ruled that selling open land without a building does not qualify for tax exemption under Section 54 of the Income Tax Act, even if the land was previously part of a residential property. The tribunal upheld the denial of exemption since no residential building was sold. This decision clarifies the scope of Section 54 exemptions, impacting taxpayers and tax administration in India.
articles.raw_summary: The tribunal held that selling only open land, even if earlier part of a residential property, does not qualify as transfer of a residential house. Since no building was sold, exemption under Section 54 was rightly denied. The post Section 54 Exemption not eligible on Sale of Open Land Without Building: ITAT Allahabad appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Recycled Activewear Reveals the Structural Limits of Fashion’s Sustainability Claims
articles.summary: The article examines the sustainability claims of recycled polyester in activewear, noting that most material still comes from PET drink bottles rather than discarded textiles. This raises concerns about whether fashion brands are truly addressing waste or merely shifting it to other recycling systems. The issue has implications for global textile waste management and could influence future environmental and trade policies in India.
articles.raw_summary: Recycled polyester has become a default sustainability claim across activewear, swimwear and outerwear, but its supply chain is less circular than its labelling suggests. Most of the material still comes from PET drink bottles, not discarded textiles, raising difficult questions about whether fashion is solving waste or diverting it from better-functioning recycling systems globally instead.

articles.title: Doctor's Legal Heirs Liable For Medical Negligence Under Consumer Protection Act : Supreme Court
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that the legal heirs of a doctor can be held liable for medical negligence under the Consumer Protection Act, but only to the extent of the doctor's estate. The Court clarified that liability does not extend beyond the inherited assets. This ruling impacts how medical negligence cases are pursued against deceased doctors' estates.
articles.raw_summary: The liability of the legal heirs is limited to the extent of the doctor's estate which devolves upon them, the Court stated.

The Vande Bharat trains are seen as a symbol of India's self-reliance in the transportation sector.

Regulators cited weak safeguards as a concern for child safety online. This action follows growing scrutiny of social media platforms' role in protecting minors. The case reflects broader global efforts to regulate digital platforms and ensure child safety in the online space.

The directive required at least one Union government employee to be present at every counting station during the state's election process. The court's decision upholds the electoral officer's mandate, which the TMC had challenged.

articles.title: Smarter fraud protection begins when you separate your money
articles.summary: India's digital payments are booming, but using a single savings account for all transactions risks your core savings. A smarter approach involves separating spending and savings. Utilize your main account for significant commitments and a Safe Second Account for daily expenses, ensuring your primary funds remain protected.

articles.title: Supreme Court to test validity of scrapping 'self-perceived gender' clause
articles.summary: The Supreme Court will review the constitutionality of the Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Amendment Act, 2026, which replaces self-perceived gender identity with medical verification. Petitions argue this contradicts the NALSA judgment's emphasis on self-determination. The court has issued notices to the Centre and states, seeking responses within six weeks.
articles.raw_summary: The Supreme Court will examine the Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Amendment Act, 2026, after petitions challenged its constitutionality. Advocates argue the new provisions, which replace self-perceived gender identity with medical verification, contradict the NALSA judgment's emphasis on self-determination. The court issued notices to the Centre and states, seeking responses within six weeks.

articles.summary: Net direct tax collections reached approximately Rs 23.40 lakh crore, marking a 5.12% increase as of March 31. The dip in refunds contributed to a stronger net revenue position.

This situation highlights potential vulnerabilities in India's energy-dependent industries and may necessitate policy interventions to mitigate supply shocks.

articles.summary: The Bombay High Court granted bail to human rights lawyer Surendra Gadling after he spent eight years in jail awaiting trial in the Bhima Koregaon case. The court's decision highlights ongoing delays in the judicial process. Gadling's case is linked to allegations under the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (UAPA), raising concerns about prolonged detention without trial. The ruling underscores issues in India's legal system regarding speedy justice and civil liberties.

articles.title: Dowry Deaths 'Serious Social Problem', Particularly In UP, Bihar & Karnataka : Supreme Court
articles.summary: The Supreme Court of India described dowry deaths as a serious social problem, particularly in Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Karnataka. The Court noted that exorbitant demands by the groom’s family often begin after marriage, exerting pressure on the bride’s family. This observation underscores the need for stronger enforcement of existing laws against dowry and related violence. The Court's remarks carry judicial weight, signaling potential legal or policy actions to address the issue.
articles.raw_summary: "Before the wedding, the groom’s family rarely makes exorbitant demands, but once the marriage is formalised, they begin exerting pressure," the Court said.

articles.summary: India is rapidly repatriating its gold reserves, bringing the majority home from overseas vaults. The move ensures immediate access to wealth during economic uncertainty, redefining safety in international finance.

articles.title: No Medical Certificate, No Disability: Sikkim HC Rejects 50% Disability Finding Based On Fractured Vertebrae
articles.summary: The Sikkim High Court rejected a 50% disability claim based on a fractured rib, vertebrae, and hip dislocation because the individual lacked a medical or disability certificate. The court ruled that without proper certification, the disability finding was invalid, reducing compensation to only medical expenses. This decision highlights the importance of formal disability documentation under Indian law.
articles.raw_summary: In the absence of medical or disability certificate, the finding of 50 per cent disability based on fracture of rib, vertebrae and hip dislocation, was found to be erroneous, reducing the award of compensation to just medical expenses. Coupled with strict government-imposed quotas limiting exports, the situation has stabilized domestic consumption rates, resulting in a reduced surplus for foreign markets.
